skill.name: azure-hosted-copilot-sdk
skill.description: Build, integrate, configure, and deploy GitHub Copilot SDK applications on Azure using Azure Developer CLI, Container Apps, and optional Azure model hosting.
skill.summary: Guides teams through Azure-hosted GitHub Copilot SDK app setup, integration, model configuration, and deployment.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but review shows they are documentation for legitimate Azure and GitHub Copilot SDK deployment workflows. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The skill remains medium risk because it guides agents through cloud deployment commands, token handling, template copying, and remote documentation lookups.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Deployment Commands Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Azure Developer CLI, Docker, GitHub CLI, Azure CLI, and curl commands. These are legitimate for building and deploying a Copilot SDK app, but they can change cloud resources, start local services, or send test requests if executed without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command instructions are present and operationally meaningful. They are expected for Azure deployment, so the risk is legitimate but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Token Handling Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill documents GitHub token retrieval, Azure credential use, Key Vault storage, and Azure model endpoint variables. This is necessary for the deployment scenario, but mishandling could expose tokens or configure privileged identities incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly discuss tokens, managed identity, Key Vault, and credential environment variables. Context shows legitimate use, but the impact of mistakes is security relevant.

medium_findings.title: Template Copying Can Modify User Projects
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to copy infrastructure, scripts, Dockerfiles, and azure.yaml from a scaffolded template into a user project. It warns against running scaffolding inside the user project, but the workflow still requires careful review to avoid overwrites or unintended deployment configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file modification workflow is documented directly. The skill includes a mitigation warning, reducing severity but not eliminating operational risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to GitHub Copilot SDK documentation, Azure endpoint examples, localhost tests, and Azure cognitive services scopes. I did not find evidence that these URLs exfiltrate credentials or target unrelated infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL contexts are documentation links, endpoint placeholders, localhost testing, and an Azure token scope. No suspicious destination or secret-bearing request was found.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are caused by markdown inline code and fenced examples, not hidden executable scripts. The skill content is instructional documentation, not code that executes automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown examples or identifiers. They do not execute unless an agent or user chooses to run the documented commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains command guidance for Azure and GitHub tooling. These patterns are appropriate for the skill domain but should be executed only after confirming target project, subscription, and deployment intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are clearly present and can affect local or cloud state. They are domain-appropriate and not evidence of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Environment Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references GitHub and Azure credential variables and token flows. This requires careful handling because generated infrastructure can store or pass secrets during deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential references are explicit, but they are tied to GitHub Copilot SDK and Azure authentication. No evidence of unauthorized transmission was found.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable guidance for turning Copilot SDK ideas into deployable Azure services. This skill guides scaffolding, integration, model setup, and deployment using proven Azure and GitHub patterns.
actual_capabilities: Routes requests between new app scaffolding, existing app deployment, SDK integration, and model configuration workflows.
Uses the Azure Samples Copilot SDK service template as the baseline for API, web, infrastructure, and token scripts.
Explains GitHub default models, named GitHub models, and Azure bring-your-own-model configuration paths.
Guides integration into Node.js, Python, Go, and .NET projects at a high level.
Documents deployment concerns for Azure Container Apps, Bicep infrastructure, Dockerfiles, Key Vault, and managed identity.
Provides troubleshooting notes for model support, token refresh, route conflicts, Docker, GitHub CLI, and gateway timeouts.
limitations: It depends on Azure Developer CLI, Docker, GitHub CLI, and valid cloud credentials being available.
It does not replace review of generated Bicep, Dockerfiles, token scripts, or azure.yaml before deployment.
Azure BYOM guidance is limited to models that support Copilot SDK encrypted prompt content.
It gives high-level integration guidance for some languages and may need current SDK documentation for exact APIs.

use_cases.title: Scaffold a new Copilot SDK service
use_cases.description: Create a greenfield Copilot SDK API and web app from the Azure sample template, then prepare it for Azure deployment.

use_cases.title: Add Copilot SDK to an existing app
use_cases.description: Identify the app framework, add the SDK dependency, create a chat endpoint, and connect authentication without duplicating the server.

use_cases.title: Configure Azure-hosted model support
use_cases.description: Set up Azure model provider values, managed identity, endpoint variables, and token refresh behavior for bring-your-own-model deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Start a new app
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new GitHub Copilot SDK app using the Azure sample template. Explain each setup step before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy an existing app
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my existing Copilot SDK app and add Azure deployment files from the template without overwriting my application code.

prompt_templates.title: Add SDK integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this project, identify its framework, and add a Copilot SDK chat endpoint that follows the existing server pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Azure BYOM
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure this Copilot SDK service to use my Azure OpenAI deployment with managed identity, token refresh, and supported model settings.

output_examples.input: Deploy my existing Copilot SDK service to Azure.
output_examples.output: A deployment plan that scaffolds the template in a temporary directory, copies infrastructure files, adapts azure.yaml, verifies token hooks, and lists validation commands.

output_examples.input: Use my Azure OpenAI deployment instead of the default model.
output_examples.output: A model configuration checklist covering endpoint variables, deployment name, managed identity, token scope, supported model family, and common BYOM errors.

output_examples.input: Add Copilot SDK chat to this app.
output_examples.output: A framework-aware integration outline with dependency changes, a new chat route, authentication setup, testing steps, and route conflict checks.

best_practices: Confirm the target Azure subscription, resource group, and repository before running deployment commands.
Read the project guidance file and inspect existing build, server, and deployment structure before editing files.
Keep token handling in secure deployment flows such as Key Vault and managed identity.
anti_patterns: Do not run scaffolding commands directly inside an existing project unless the user explicitly approves overwrites.
Do not paste GitHub or Azure tokens into source files, logs, or chat responses.
Do not use unsupported Azure BYOM models when encrypted Copilot SDK prompt content is required.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build, integrate, configure, and deploy GitHub Copilot SDK applications on Azure.

faq.question: Can it deploy an app I already have?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides copying template infrastructure into an existing Copilot SDK app and adapting deployment settings.

faq.question: Does it support Azure OpenAI models?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents Azure bring-your-own-model setup with supported models, endpoints, managed identity, and token refresh guidance.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready infrastructure automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and template-based files that must be reviewed before production deployment.

faq.question: Which tools do I need locally?
faq.answer: Typical workflows need Azure Developer CLI, Docker, GitHub CLI, cloud credentials, and project-specific package tooling.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses legitimate deployment and credential workflows, but those workflows can affect cloud resources and secrets.
